planting.note: Sow scarified seed in spring in well-drained soil in full to partial sun.

directions: Scarify and sow the seed a quarter inch deep in average, well-drained soil. This southeastern native forms softly hairy leafy stems and settles in for the long haul once its taproot is set. Bright yellow flower spikes appear in late spring.

planting.note: Sow scarified seed in spring in sharply drained, gritty soil in full sun.

directions: Scarify the seed and sow shallowly in very well-drained, rocky soil, as this high-altitude Himalayan species dislikes wet feet. Woolly, silvery foliage clothes the low stems. Unusual purple to violet pea-flowers open in early summer, distinct from the yellow-flowered members of the genus.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in a sunny, well-drained bed that mimics its prairie habitat.

directions: Sow shallowly into gritty, fast-draining soil and avoid overwatering established plants. This drought-tolerant prairie species handles heat and poor ground with ease. Blue-violet flowers with bright yellow anthers open in the morning through late spring and summer.

planting.note: Sow or divide in spring into humus-rich soil in dappled woodland shade.

directions: Keep seeds and young plants moist in a shaded, woodsy bed with plenty of leaf mold. Recognized by its kinked, zigzagging stems, this species blooms better with a little sun. Deep blue flowers appear from midsummer into early fall, later than most spiderworts.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in full sun and lean soil like its native prairies.

directions: Sow into well-drained ground and let seedlings establish deep roots before summer heat. This compact prairie native has hairy stems and leafy bracts beneath the flowers. Rose-purple to blue blooms open in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions after the last frost where the golden foliage lights up a border.

directions: Set divisions at the depth they grew, spacing them for good air flow and mulching to hold moisture. The chartreuse-gold leaves scorch in harsh afternoon sun, so give light shade in hot regions. Deep blue flowers glow against the bright foliage from late spring through summer; shear back after the first flush for rebloom.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, fertile soil.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil and keep new plantings evenly watered while they establish. Blue-green foliage carries a silvery cast that sets off the flowers. Rich grape-purple blooms open each morning from late spring into summer; cut back spent stems to freshen the clump.

planting.note: Plant divisions after frost in a sunny to lightly shaded bed.

directions: Plant divisions in fertile, moisture-retentive soil and water through dry spells. This classic selection is prized for its unusually large flowers. Broad royal-blue blooms with golden stamens appear from early summer onward; deadhead or shear to prolong the display.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist soil and partial shade.

directions: Set divisions at soil level and keep them watered while roots take hold. The grassy blue-green clumps look tidy in a mixed border or beside a pond. White flowers centered with fluffy blue stamens open in the morning through summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions after frost in fertile, evenly moist ground.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level and mulch to conserve moisture. Regular water keeps foliage lush and blooming strong. Vivid reddish-purple flowers appear from late spring into summer; a midseason cutback tidies the clump and spurs fresh bloom.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny to part-shade border.

directions: Set divisions level with the soil and water regularly during establishment. The mounding habit fits well toward the front of a perennial planting. Bright magenta-purple flowers cover the clump through early and midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions after frost in moist, humus-rich soil.

directions: Plant divisions at the depth they grew and keep the soil evenly moist. The clean white flowers brighten a shady corner or a woodland edge. Large pure-white blooms open each morning through the summer months.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, fertile soil.

directions: Set divisions at soil level and provide steady moisture as they establish. This pure white selection stays fresh-looking in dappled light. White flowers with yellow stamens appear from late spring into summer; shear after the first flush to encourage more.

planting.note: Plant divisions after frost in fertile, moist ground.

directions: Plant divisions at the crown line and keep well watered for the brightest color. This German selection, also sold as Carmine Glow, stands out among the blues and purples. Glowing carmine-red flowers open through summer; a light cutback keeps bloom coming.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny to part-shade bed.

directions: Set divisions level with the soil and water regularly through the first season. Prized for its large, richly colored flowers on tidy clumps. Deep Oxford-blue blooms with contrasting stamens appear from early summer on.

planting.note: Plant divisions after frost in moist, fertile soil.

directions: Plant divisions at their original depth and keep evenly moist while rooting. This old favorite is noted for extra-large flowers on strong clumps. Soft china-blue blooms open in the morning from early through late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in evenly moist ground.

directions: Set divisions level with the soil and mulch to hold moisture. Grassy clumps stay attractive between flushes of bloom. Violet-blue flowers appear through summer; cut plants back after the first wave for a second show.

planting.note: Plant divisions after frost in moist, fertile soil.

directions: Plant divisions at the depth they grew and keep watered during establishment. The soft flower color blends easily into cottage and mixed borders. Orchid-pink to mauve blooms open each morning from early summer onward.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, fertile soil.

directions: Set divisions level with the soil and water steadily as they settle in. The bicolor flowers add a fresh, cool note to the border. Pale lilac-white petals with a deeper violet eye and pink centers open through summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions after frost near the front of a bed where its compact size shows.

directions: Plant divisions at soil level and keep evenly moist while they establish. This dwarf selection suits small gardens and edging. Soft light-blue flowers cover the low mound from late spring through summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, fertile ground.

directions: Set divisions level with the soil and provide regular water during establishment. The rosy blooms warm up plantings of blue and purple spiderworts. Bright rose-pink flowers open through the summer; deadhead to keep the clump neat.

planting.note: Plant divisions after frost in a sunny to part-shade bed.

directions: Plant divisions at the depth they grew and water regularly to establish. Strong clumps of grassy foliage carry the flowers well. Pure blue blooms open each morning from early summer into fall with a midseason trim.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, fertile soil.

directions: Set divisions level with the soil and keep evenly moist during establishment. The soft pastel flowers suit a cottage-style planting. Delicate lilac-pink blooms open in the morning through summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions after frost in moist, humus-rich soil.

directions: Plant divisions at their original depth and water steadily while rooting. The subtle two-tone flowers read almost pearly in dappled light. White petals flushed with pale blue open each morning through summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in fertile, moist ground.

directions: Set divisions level with the soil and mulch to keep roots cool and moist. The saturated flower color holds up well through the season. Deep reddish-purple blooms open from early summer onward; cut back midseason to refresh.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings once nights are reliably warm and frost has passed.

directions: Root a stem cutting in moist soil, where it takes hold within a couple of weeks, and plant where it can sprawl. Full sun deepens the vivid purple foliage, while shade turns it greener. Small pink three-petaled flowers appear amid the leaves through the warm months; in cold zones grow it as a houseplant or annual and overwinter cuttings indoors.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost in a warm, sunny spot or container.

directions: Root cuttings in moist mix and plant out once the weather is settled and warm. Bright light brings out the pink pinstriping over the purple leaves. Pale pink flowers open along the trailing stems in summer; bring cuttings indoors before frost in cold climates.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost, or grow year-round as a houseplant.

directions: Push a cutting into moist soil where it roots readily within a week or two. Bright indirect light keeps the silver-and-purple leaf stripes vivid, while too much shade fades them. Pinch stem tips to keep growth full; small lavender-pink flowers may appear, and it trails handsomely from pots or baskets.

planting.note: Plant cuttings after frost, or grow indoors in bright light all year.

directions: Root a cutting in damp soil and give it strong indirect light for the deepest color. This selection carries darker, wine-toned foliage than the species. Pinch regularly for bushy growth; it spills gracefully over the edge of a container.

planting.note: Plant cuttings after frost, or keep as a bright-light houseplant.

directions: Root cuttings in moist mix and provide plenty of bright, indirect light to hold the variegation. Leaves are banded in green, silver, pink, and cream. Trim out any all-green shoots to keep the multicolor pattern, and pinch tips for fullness.

planting.note: Plant cuttings after frost in shade, or grow as a houseplant; can be invasive in mild climates.

directions: Lay or insert cuttings on moist soil and they root quickly at the nodes. It thrives in shade and moisture and spreads fast, so contain it where winters are mild. Tiny white flowers dot the trailing stems; keep it in bounds or grow it in a hanging pot.

planting.note: Plant cuttings after frost in part shade, or grow indoors in bright light.

directions: Root cuttings in moist soil and give bright, indirect light to keep the stripes crisp. Leaves are streaked green, white, and pink. Pinch tips for density and remove reverted green stems; it trails nicely from a basket.

planting.note: Plant cuttings after frost in a warm, sunny, well-drained spot or pot.

directions: Root cuttings in gritty, fast-draining mix and water sparingly, as this is the most succulent-like spiderwort. Dense white woolly hairs cover the stems and leaves, giving a silvery look. Bright magenta-pink flowers open at the stem tips in summer; let the soil dry between waterings.

planting.note: Plant offsets or divisions after frost, or grow as a houseplant year-round.

directions: Plant offsets in well-drained soil and give bright light for the strongest leaf color. Stiff rosettes show green above and purple below. Small white flowers nestle in boat-shaped purple bracts at the leaf bases; it clumps up steadily and can be divided.

planting.note: Plant offsets after frost in a bright spot, or grow indoors as a houseplant.

directions: Plant offsets in well-drained soil and provide bright light to keep the stripes vivid. Rosettes are striped in green, cream, and pink above with purple undersides. Little white flowers sit in purple bracts among the leaves; divide clumps to make more plants.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost, or grow as a bright-light houseplant.

directions: Root a cutting in moist soil and give it plenty of bright, indirect light for strong pink coloring. This sturdy, thick-leaved selection shows green, cream, and bubblegum-pink stripes. Pinch tips to keep it bushy; small pale flowers may appear, and it looks best in a pot.

planting.note: Plant cuttings after frost in part shade, or grow indoors in bright light.

directions: Root cuttings in moist soil, where they take hold quickly at the nodes. The fleshy leaves are green above with purple undersides and fine hairs along the margins. Small pink-and-white flowers open in clusters; pinch to keep the trailing stems full.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring in a shaded bed enriched with leaf mold.

directions: Plant the crown level with the soil in humus-rich, consistently moist ground beneath high shade. Mulch to keep roots cool and never let the soil dry out during summer. Arching stems open orchid-like white flowers heavily speckled purple from late summer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in cool, shaded woodland soil.

directions: Give it a woodland spot with loose, leafy soil that holds moisture without staying soggy. Keep it out of harsh afternoon sun to protect the foliage from scorch. Branched stems carry small white flowers freckled purple-brown in late summer.

planting.note: Set out in spring in shade with steady soil moisture.

directions: Plant in fertile, humusy soil in a shaded border where the broad leaves will not wilt in midday heat. Water regularly and mulch to hold moisture around the shallow roots. This early bloomer opens yellow-green flowers spotted purple in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring and plant in shaded, moist ground.

directions: Tuck into a shady woodland pocket with rich, moisture-retentive soil and shelter from wind. Keep the soil evenly damp and refresh mulch yearly. White flowers marked with purple spots nod from leaf axils in late summer and early autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered, shaded spot after frost danger passes.

directions: Choose a warm, wind-protected shade spot with rich, moist soil for this tall late-season species. Provide extra winter mulch at the colder edge of its range. Its branching stems bear clusters of purple-spotted flowers very late in the season, often into mid-autumn.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring where arching stems can cascade.

directions: Grow this arching species on a shaded bank or wall top so the trailing stems can spill downward. Keep the humus-rich soil cool and consistently moist. Pendant, waxy yellow bell-shaped flowers spotted brown-purple hang beneath the stems in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring at the front of a shaded bed.

directions: Place this compact species at the edge of a shade bed or in a woodland trough with gritty, humus-rich soil. Keep it evenly moist but never waterlogged. Large upward-facing yellow flowers speckled brown open close to the ground in early to mid autumn.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring in cool, shaded soil.

directions: Grow this short species in dappled shade with loose, leafy, reliably moist soil. Guard it from drought and strong sun that can brown the foliage. Bright yellow flowers lightly spotted purple appear on low stems in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a shaded rock garden or trough.

directions: Use this miniature toad lily in a shaded rock garden or container with sharply drained yet moisture-holding soil. Keep it cool at the root and water in dry weather. Small yellow flowers dotted purple sit low among the foliage in early autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring where arching stems can arch outward or trail.

directions: Grow this species with clasping perfoliate leaves on a shaded ledge where the arching stems can drape. Keep the humus-rich soil cool and moist. Yellow flowers spotted red-brown are cradled along the leafy stems in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sheltered shade spot.

directions: Site this tender Taiwanese species in a warm, protected shade bed with rich, steadily moist soil. Mulch well and avoid cold, exposed positions. Reddish-purple to brownish flowers open on arching stems very late in the growing season.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in rich, shaded, moist soil.

directions: Set the crown at soil level in humus-rich, evenly moist ground in part to full shade. Keep watered through summer and mulch to shade the roots. Long, gracefully arching stems line up white flowers heavily peppered with lilac spots from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, shaded, humus-rich soil.

directions: Grow in dappled shade with fertile, consistently moist soil and steady mulch. Keep it out of drying afternoon sun. This selection brightens shade with pure white, largely unspotted flowers along arching stems in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a shaded, moisture-retentive bed.

directions: Plant in rich, damp woodland soil in part shade and mulch to conserve moisture. Water during dry summer stretches to prevent leaf scorch. Clean white flowers face upward from the leaf axils along erect stems in late summer and fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in bright shade to hold leaf variegation.

directions: Give this form bright, filtered shade so the creamy leaf margins stay crisp without scorching. Keep the rich soil evenly moist and mulched. Purple-spotted white flowers open above the gold-edged foliage from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in filtered shade for best leaf color.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, moist soil in bright shade where the gold leaf edges will read well. Water steadily and mulch to keep the roots cool. Arching stems carry lilac-speckled white flowers over the golden-margined leaves in autumn.

planting.note: Plant robust divisions in spring in rich, moist shade.

directions: Plant this vigorous, spot-free hybrid in fertile, moist soil in part shade where it forms a substantial clump. It tolerates a bit more sun than most if kept moist. Large lavender-blue flowers with pale throats open freely from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in bright shade to keep the gold leaf edges.

directions: Grow in rich, evenly moist soil in filtered shade so the fine gold leaf margins stay bright. Water in dry weather and mulch to hold moisture. Purple-spotted lilac-pink flowers rise above the variegated foliage in early autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in bright, cool shade.

directions: Set into humus-rich, moist soil in dappled shade where the yellow-edged leaves show off. Keep the soil damp and refresh mulch each spring. Star-shaped flowers of pink and purple spotting appear above the bright foliage in fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, shaded ground.

directions: Plant this sturdy upright hybrid in rich, consistently moist soil in part shade. Mulch and water through summer to keep it thriving. Dense clusters of white flowers heavily spotted red-purple open along the stems in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in bright shade to display the streaked leaves.

directions: Grow in rich, moist soil in filtered shade so the yellow-streaked leaves keep their contrast. Water regularly and avoid hot, dry exposures. Lavender flowers spotted purple open above the boldly striped foliage in late summer and fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, fertile shade.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, reliably moist soil in part shade and mulch to conserve water. Keep it evenly watered for the fullest flush. Large white flowers boldly marked with purple spots open in generous clusters through autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, moist, shaded soil.

directions: Site in rich, moist soil in part to full shade and keep the roots cool with mulch. Water through dry summer weather to keep foliage fresh. Upward-facing flowers in cool lavender-blue tones open along the stems in early autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, humus-rich shade.

directions: Plant this compact hybrid in fertile, evenly moist soil in dappled shade. Mulch and water steadily to support the heavy bloom. Richly colored raspberry-purple flowers with darker spotting cover the low, tidy clump in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in bright shade for the best gold margins.

directions: Grow in rich, moist soil in filtered shade so the gold-margined leaves glow without burning. Keep watered and mulched through the season. Purple-spotted white flowers open over the variegated foliage in early to mid autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in bright, moist shade to keep the marbled foliage.

directions: Set into humus-rich, evenly moist soil in filtered shade where the cream-marbled leaves stay unscorched. Water and mulch to keep roots cool. Lilac-pink flowers dotted with purple rise above the boldly variegated foliage in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, shaded, fertile soil.

directions: Plant this spreading hybrid in rich, moist soil in part shade where it can form a broad drift. Water regularly and mulch to hold moisture. Dusky mauve-purple flowers with a satiny sheen and darker speckling open in generous sprays in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, moist, shaded ground.

directions: Grow this botanical variety in rich, evenly moist soil in dappled shade. Water through summer and mulch to keep the shallow roots cool. Flowers show unusually clear amethyst-blue coloring with a white, red-spotted throat in early autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, shaded, humus-rich soil.

directions: Plant in fertile, consistently moist soil in part shade and mulch to conserve moisture. Keep it watered through dry spells for the best display. White flowers with a soft ring of purple markings toward the center open along arching stems in fall.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in autumn so winter cold breaks dormancy, or cold-stratify before spring sowing.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and keep the medium cool and constantly moist, expecting slow and uneven sprouting. Site plants in humus-rich soil that never dries out, ideally beside a pond or in a damp border. The rounded lemon-yellow globes open in late spring and last for weeks in cool conditions.

planting.note: Autumn sowing lets natural cold satisfy the seed's chilling requirement.

directions: Press seed onto a cool, moist surface and shade the tray until sprouts appear over several weeks. Grow in consistently damp, fertile ground and divide congested clumps every few years to keep vigor. Look for the more open, upward-facing orange-gold cups on tall stems in early summer.

planting.note: Cold-stratify or sow fresh in fall to trigger germination.

directions: Surface-sow into a gritty, moisture-retentive mix and hold it cool while seedlings emerge slowly. Keep plants in reliably wet soil at the front of a bog or streamside planting. Deep tangerine-orange globes appear in late spring above deeply cut foliage.

planting.note: A mountain species that needs a cold spell before it will sprout.

directions: Sow fresh seed and expose the pot to winter cold outdoors to break dormancy. Plant in cool, damp, humus-rich soil in a spot sheltered from hot afternoon sun. Orange-yellow cups with a darker center open in early summer.

planting.note: Sow in autumn for natural chilling and spring emergence.

directions: Keep the sowing medium cool and evenly moist until the slow seedlings appear. Grow in a damp woodland-edge setting with rich, retentive soil. This species opens flatter, buttercup-like golden flowers in early to midsummer.

planting.note: A compact alpine that germinates after cold stratification.

directions: Sow fine seed onto gritty, moist compost and chill the pot over winter. This low grower suits a rock garden or trough with cool, damp roots. Shining yellow single flowers sit just above the mat of leaves in early summer.

planting.note: High-altitude species requiring a cold period to sprout.

directions: Surface-sow and overwinter the pot outdoors to satisfy the chilling need. Plant in cool, gritty but moisture-retentive soil in an alpine bed. Large open golden cups appear almost stemless above the foliage in late spring.

planting.note: A native wetland species; sow fresh in fall for cold conditioning.

directions: Sow fresh seed into constantly wet, humus-rich medium and keep it cool. This bog and wet-meadow native must never dry out. Pale creamy-yellow, more open flowers appear in spring.

planting.note: Western North American native; sow in fall for natural chilling.

directions: Keep the seed pot cool and wet through winter to prompt spring germination. Grow in consistently moist mountain-meadow conditions with rich soil. Creamy-white, open flowers with prominent stamens open soon after snowmelt.

planting.note: Set out divisions or potted plants in early spring while the soil is cool and moist.

directions: Plant with the crown level with the soil in a rich, reliably damp bed and mulch to hold moisture. Cut back spent stems after flowering to tidy the clump and sometimes coax a light rebloom. Soft lemon-yellow globes crowd the stems in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring into cool, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Site in humus-rich ground that stays damp, adding compost at planting to feed the hungry roots. Keep well watered through dry spells and divide clumps every few years to maintain flowering. Deep golden-orange globes rise on strong stems in late spring.

planting.note: Best planted in spring in a cool, partly shaded, damp spot.

directions: Give this pale selection a sheltered position with light shade so the soft color does not scorch. Keep the soil consistently moist and rich, mulching in spring. The unusual ivory to pale primrose globes open in early summer and glow in shadier corners.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into damp, fertile ground.

directions: Work plenty of organic matter into the planting hole and keep the roots cool and moist. Deadhead after the main flush to keep the plant looking neat. Pale butter-yellow, softly rounded flowers appear in late spring above lush divided leaves.

planting.note: Set out in early spring in reliably moist soil.

directions: Plant in rich, damp ground and never let the roots dry through the growing season. Remove faded flowers to encourage a modest second show. Clear lemon-yellow cups top slender stems from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring for the earliest globeflower bloom of the season.

directions: Choose a cool, moist border and enrich the soil with compost before planting. Keep watered and mulched so it never checks in dry weather. True to its name, warm yellow globes appear noticeably ahead of other globeflowers in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in deep, damp, fertile soil.

directions: Give this tall selection a bog margin or damp border with rich soil and support if the site is windy. Keep constantly moist and divide when clumps crowd. Open, upward-facing deep orange-gold flowers with prominent stamens appear in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring into rich, moisture-holding soil.

directions: Plant in a cool, damp bed with generous organic matter and mulch each spring. Water freely in dry weather and cut back after flowering. Fiery deep orange globes make a strong show in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a vigorous, tall cultivar for the back of a damp border.

directions: Allow room for this large, robust selection and plant in deep, rich, constantly moist soil. Keep well watered and divide every few years to sustain flowering. Big golden-yellow globes rise on strong tall stems in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a cool, damp, part-shaded spot.

directions: Enrich the soil at planting and keep the roots cool and evenly moist all season. Deadhead spent blooms to keep the clump tidy. Bright canary-yellow globes open above finely divided foliage in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring into fertile, moisture-retentive ground.

directions: Grow in a damp border or beside water where the soil never bakes dry. Mulch in spring and water through summer heat. Soft pale yellow globes with a cooler, moonlit tone open in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in rich, damp soil for a bold double-flowered display.

directions: Plant in a moisture-retentive border enriched with compost and keep it well watered. Remove faded flowers to keep the plant looking fresh. Fully double, fiery orange blooms crowd the stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring into deep, moist, fertile soil.

directions: Site in a cool, damp position with rich soil and steady moisture through summer. Cut spent stems to the base after the flush. Deep, glowing orange globes make one of the darkest displays among globeflowers in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, damp, humus-rich soil.

directions: Give this soft-toned selection light shade and consistently moist, fertile ground. Mulch to conserve water and deadhead after flowering. Gentle creamy pale yellow globes open in late spring and suit cooler color schemes.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a tall, large-flowered selection for damp borders.

directions: Allow generous spacing and plant in deep, rich, reliably moist soil. Keep watered in dry spells and divide when the clump becomes crowded. Large golden-yellow globes are held high on sturdy stems in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant this old, reliable cultivar in a cool, damp bed rich in organic matter. Water freely and cut back spent stems after the main flush. Rich deep orange globes appear on strong stems from late spring into early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, damp, fertile ground.

directions: Site in reliably moist soil enriched with compost and keep the roots from drying out. Deadhead to tidy the clump after flowering. Large bright golden-yellow globes light up the border in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring into damp, humus-rich soil.

directions: Grow in a cool border or bog edge where the soil stays consistently moist. Mulch in spring and water through summer dry spells. Warm golden-orange globes open freely in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Start indoors or sow outdoors in spring; blooms in the second or third year.

directions: Sow the tiny seed on the surface and press it in gently, keeping it barely moist until it germinates. Set young plants in poor, gritty, well-drained soil where the large silvery rosettes have room to spread. Branching candelabras of golden-yellow flowers rise well overhead in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Grown for silver foliage the first year and a tall spike the next.

directions: Surface sow and do not cover the seed, which needs light to sprout, then keep it just damp. Plant into lean, fast-draining soil in full sun so the felted white rosette stays dry at the crown. Woolly stems clad in white hairs carry soft yellow flowers in the second summer.

planting.note: Started early indoors, it often flowers in its first summer.

directions: Surface sow indoors eight weeks before the last frost and keep warm and evenly moist. Transplant into lean, well-drained soil in full sun, spacing plants for good airflow. Wiry stems carry flowers in shades of violet, rose, and white from late spring into summer.

planting.note: A reliable short-lived perennial that self-sows freely.

directions: Sow the seed on the surface and press it in lightly, keeping it just moist until it sprouts. Grow in average to poor, well-drained soil in full sun or very light shade. Dense spikes of yellow flowers with fuzzy purple-red filament centers bloom over a long summer season.

planting.note: Forms a first-year rosette and flowers the following year.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface in spring and keep it lightly moist until seedlings appear. Site in lean, dry, well-drained soil in full sun where it can naturalize. Airy stems bear five-petaled white or pale yellow flowers with fuzzy orange-and-purple centers in early summer.

planting.note: Traditional herbal mullein grown for its dense yellow spike.

directions: Surface sow in spring and leave the seed uncovered, as light triggers germination. Grow in poor, dry, well-drained soil and full sun for the sturdiest spikes. Crowded golden-yellow flowers open densely up the tall stem in the second summer.

planting.note: Branching mullein with small pale flowers on a mealy stem.

directions: Press seed onto the soil surface in spring and keep barely moist until it germinates. Choose dry, chalky or gravelly ground in full sun where drainage is sharp. Loose, branched panicles of small white to pale yellow flowers appear in midsummer of the second year.

planting.note: Large-flowered woolly mullein, biennial in most gardens.

directions: Surface sow in spring, leaving the seed exposed to light, and keep the bed just damp. Plant in lean, well-drained soil in full sun for the strongest stems. Broad yellow, faintly orange-tinted flowers open along the tall spike in the second summer.

planting.note: Selected for especially white, felted foliage and tall spikes.

directions: Sow on the surface without covering and keep lightly moist until germination. Grow in gritty, fast-draining soil in full sun so the silvery rosette does not rot at the crown. Pale yellow flowers stud the striking white woolly spikes in the second summer.

planting.note: The German name for the same silver-leaved selection as Arctic Summer.

directions: Surface sow the fine seed, leave it uncovered for light, and keep just damp. Set plants into lean, sharply drained soil in full sun for the whitest foliage. Soft yellow flowers rise on tall white-felted stems in the plant's second year.

planting.note: A white-flowered form of the long-lived nettle-leaved mullein.

directions: Sow on the surface and keep lightly moist until seedlings emerge, then grow on for planting in spring. Give average, well-drained soil in full sun and cut spent spikes for a second flush. Slim spikes of white flowers accented by fuzzy mauve centers bloom through summer.

planting.note: A classic Cotswold hybrid best grown from root cuttings or purchased plants.

directions: Set plants or rooted root cuttings into lean, well-drained soil in full sun at the same depth they were growing. Avoid rich, damp ground, which shortens the life of this short-lived perennial. Bronze-amber flowers with dusky purple eyes climb the tall spikes through early and midsummer.

planting.note: Soft biscuit-buff flowers on a classic Cotswold Group hybrid.

directions: Plant nursery stock or root cuttings in gritty, well-drained soil and full sun. Keep it on the dry side and stake tall spikes on windy sites. Warm buff-apricot flowers with mauve centers open up the stems from early summer.

planting.note: One of the best-known hybrid mulleins, grown from root cuttings.

directions: Set plants into free-draining soil in full sun and avoid overly rich beds. Deadhead the main spike to encourage side shoots and prolong flowering. Long spikes of soft primrose-yellow flowers rise above gray-green foliage all summer.

planting.note: A rose-pink hybrid mullein propagated by root cuttings.

directions: Plant into lean, well-drained soil in full sun, setting the crown level with the surface. Water sparingly once established and remove faded spikes for repeat bloom. Deep rosy-pink flowers with darker eyes cover the branching stems in summer.

planting.note: A white-flowered Cotswold-type hybrid mullein.

directions: Plant in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun and keep the crown dry. Cut back the first spike as it fades to draw out later flowers. Pure white flowers with faint mauve centers rise on tall gray-felted stems in summer.

planting.note: A famous coppery hybrid, sterile and grown from root cuttings.

directions: Set plants or root cuttings into sharply drained soil in full sun and avoid winter wet. Trim off spent spikes to keep this short-lived perennial flowering longer. Unusual coppery buff-and-dusky-pink flowers open on upright stems through summer.

planting.note: A compact peach-flowered hybrid ideal for the front of a border.

directions: Plant into well-drained soil in full sun, keeping this dwarf mullein toward the border edge. Deadhead regularly to prolong a long summer display. Warm apricot-peach flowers with dusky centers cover short, tidy spikes.

planting.note: A tall apricot-orange hybrid grown from root cuttings.

directions: Set plants into lean, free-draining soil in full sun and stake the tall spikes if needed. Cut back after the main flush to encourage further bloom. Glowing apricot-orange flowers open densely up the stately spikes in early summer.

planting.note: A cherry-toned sport in the Helen Johnson style, grown from cuttings.

directions: Plant into sharply drained soil in full sun and avoid rich, soggy conditions. Remove faded flower spikes to encourage a longer season. Dusky cherry-pink flowers with deeper eyes open on upright stems through summer.

planting.note: A deep violet purple mullein that can bloom the first year from an early sowing.

directions: Surface sow indoors in late winter and keep warm and evenly moist until germination. Transplant into well-drained soil in full sun after frost. Slender spikes of rich deep-violet flowers rise above a low rosette from late spring.

planting.note: A pure white selection of purple mullein, easily raised from seed.

directions: Sow on the surface indoors about eight weeks before last frost and keep just moist. Set plants into lean, well-drained soil in full sun. Airy spikes of clean white flowers appear above the ground-hugging rosette in early summer.

planting.note: A rose-pink form of purple mullein grown from seed.

directions: Surface sow indoors in late winter and keep warm until seedlings emerge. Transplant into free-draining soil in full sun once frost has passed. Slim spikes of soft rose-pink flowers rise from the flat rosette in late spring and summer.

planting.note: A seed strain in soft pastel blends that often blooms the first year.

directions: Sow on the surface indoors and keep warm and lightly moist until germination. Move seedlings to well-drained soil in full sun after the last frost. Spikes open in muted shades of buff, lavender, cream, and dusky rose from early summer.

planting.note: A creamy soft-yellow strain that flowers in its first summer from an early start.

directions: Surface sow indoors in late winter and keep warm until seedlings appear. Plant out into lean, well-drained soil in full sun after frost. Soft custard-yellow flowers with a faint pink flush open up the spikes through summer.

planting.note: A compact mauve-purple hybrid grown from root cuttings.

directions: Plant into sharply drained soil in full sun and keep on the dry side. Deadhead often to sustain a long bloom period on this short-lived perennial. Purple-mauve flowers with darker centers cover the tidy spikes through summer.

planting.note: A compact rose-and-plum hybrid propagated from root cuttings.

directions: Set plants into well-drained soil in full sun and avoid rich, wet ground. Remove spent spikes to encourage more flowers on this front-of-border plant. Dusky rose flowers with plum-purple eyes open on neat upright stems in summer.

planting.note: A soft pink hybrid mullein grown from root cuttings.

directions: Plant into gritty, well-drained soil in full sun and keep the crown dry over winter. Cut back after the first flush to draw out later spikes. Pretty soft-pink flowers with subtle darker eyes open along upright stems in summer.

planting.note: A raspberry-and-cream hybrid mullein grown from cuttings.

directions: Set plants into free-draining soil in full sun and avoid winter wet. Deadhead spent spikes to keep the display going through summer. Blended raspberry-pink and cream flowers with dark eyes cover the branching stems.

planting.note: A statuesque silver-leaved yellow selection linked to Great Dixter.

directions: Surface sow the fine seed and leave it uncovered, keeping it just damp until germination. Grow in poor, well-drained soil in full sun to keep the tall spikes sturdy. Branching candelabras of bright yellow flowers tower over silvery foliage in summer.

planting.note: A dwarf shrubby alpine hybrid grown from cuttings for a rock garden or trough.

directions: Plant into very gritty, sharply drained soil in full sun, ideal for a raised bed or trough. Keep it lean and dry, as this sterile alpine hybrid resents damp winters. Masses of small clear-yellow flowers cover the twiggy gray mound through early summer.

planting.note: A white-flowered form of dark mullein with contrasting purple centers.

directions: Surface sow the seed, pressing it in lightly, and keep just moist until it germinates. Grow in average, well-drained soil in full sun or light shade. Spikes of white flowers set off by fuzzy purple filament centers bloom over a long summer.

planting.note: A compact selection best sown indoors early for bloom the first year.

directions: Surface-sow onto warm, moist mix and leave uncovered, thinning to the strongest seedlings once true leaves form. This dwarf form keeps the same lavender-purple clusters on shorter, self-supporting stems that rarely need staking. Expect flowers from early summer onward with light deadheading to keep them coming.

planting.note: Set out vegetatively grown plants after frost danger has passed.

directions: Plant nursery starts at their original depth in fast-draining soil and full sun. This branching selection stays lower and blooms without the flopping of the wild species, so staking is seldom needed. Purple flower heads appear from late spring and continue nonstop into autumn.

planting.note: Start early indoors; the tuberous roots overwinter in mild zones.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in warm mix and keep it lightly moist until sprouts appear. Give plants sharp drainage and full sun, where their spreading tubers form colonies over time. Stiff stems carry rounded heads of violet-purple flowers from early summer to frost.

planting.note: A uniform seed strain of slender vervain grown as a warm-season perennial.

directions: Press seed lightly into warm mix and keep evenly moist through the slow germination window. Plants tolerate heat and dry soil once established and reward full sun with dense flowering. Rich violet-purple clusters open across summer and hold up well as cut stems.

planting.note: A pale ice-blue selection of slender vervain for the front of a border.

directions: Sow onto warm, moist mix and cover very lightly, thinning once seedlings are sturdy. Site in full sun with lean, well-drained soil and give little supplemental water once rooted. Soft silvery-lavender flower heads appear over a long summer season and pair well with hotter colors.

planting.note: A soft-pink form of the native; cold stratification improves germination.

directions: Sow onto moist soil and expose the seed to a period of cold to trigger sprouting. Grow in full sun in moist to wet ground where the tall spikes stand upright without support. Slender candelabra spikes carry pale rose flowers through summer and feed bees and butterflies.

planting.note: A white-flowered native selection best fall-sown for natural chilling.

directions: Surface-sow into damp ground and allow cold, moist conditions to ready the seed for spring. Plants thrive in full sun and moist soils and hold their narrow flowering spires without staking. Clean white flowers rise on branched candelabras through mid and late summer.

planting.note: A drought-tough prairie native; sow in fall for cold stratification.

directions: Sow seed on the surface of lean ground and let winter cold satisfy its dormancy requirement. This prairie native handles dry, poor soils and full sun once its deep roots establish. Woolly gray leaves back stiff spikes of blue-purple flowers that bloom from the base upward across summer.

planting.note: A low native trailer sometimes still sold as Verbena canadensis.

directions: Sow indoors into warm mix and be patient, as this native germinates slowly and unevenly. Set plants in full sun with sharp drainage where trailing stems root as they spread into a low mat. Fragrant rose-purple clusters cover the plant in spring and repeat through the season with occasional shearing.

planting.note: A vigorous cutting-grown trailer set out after frost.

directions: Plant rooted starts at soil level in full sun with excellent drainage and space them to spread. This tough hybrid mats quickly and shrugs off heat and humidity better than most trailing verbenas. Deep magenta-purple flowers blanket the plant from late spring until frost; shear lightly midseason to renew bloom.

planting.note: An upright high-country vervain hardier than the tall South American species.

directions: Start seed in warm mix and grow on until roots fill the cell before planting out. Give full sun and dry, well-drained soil where the sturdy upright stems stand without staking. Slim spikes of soft lavender flowers rise above the foliage through summer and dry to attractive seed heads.

planting.note: An old herbal vervain grown as a short-lived hardy perennial.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm mix and thin seedlings once they are sturdy enough to handle. Plants take dry, poor soils in full sun and often self-sow to persist in the garden. Wiry branched stems carry tiny pale-lilac flowers through summer above deeply cut lower leaves.

planting.note: A finely cut trailing verbena grown as a perennial in mild regions.

directions: Start seed indoors in warm mix and keep barely moist until sprouts appear. Set out in full sun with fast drainage where the lacy stems spread into a low carpet. Purple, lavender, or white flower clusters cover the ferny foliage from spring through fall and it frequently reseeds.

planting.note: A white trailing hybrid propagated from cuttings and set out after frost.

directions: Plant rooted starts level with the soil in full sun and let the stems trail and root as they run. This finely cut hybrid stays low and spreads widely, making a cool white edging or basket filler. Pure white flower clusters appear from spring to frost with light shearing to keep it tidy.

planting.note: A classic bright-pink trailing hybrid grown from cuttings.

directions: Set rooted plants at soil level in full sun with sharp drainage and room to trail. This vigorous hybrid spreads into a low mat and blooms hardest where summers are warm and bright. Vivid rose-pink flower heads appear from late spring through fall with occasional deadheading.

planting.note: A fragrant pink-fading-to-silver trailer grown from cuttings.

directions: Plant rooted starts in full sun with free-draining soil and let the stems spread outward. The scented flowers open rich pink and soften to pale silvery-pink as they age, giving a two-tone effect across the mat. Blooms carry from early summer to frost and respond to light shearing with fresh flushes.

planting.note: A cold-tough series bred to overwinter into cooler zones than most trailers.

directions: Plant vegetative starts level in full sun with well-drained soil and space to mound and spread. This heat- and cold-tolerant series keeps flowering through midsummer heat when many verbenas stall. Clusters of blue-violet flowers cover the plant from spring to frost with minimal deadheading.

planting.note: A two-tone pink form of the hardy EnduraScape series.

directions: Set rooted starts at soil level in a sunny, well-drained bed and water sparingly once established. The series is bred for heat and cold endurance and holds up through the hottest weeks of summer. Pink-and-white bicolor flower clusters bloom continuously from spring until hard frost.

planting.note: A saturated magenta form of the hardy trailing series.

directions: Plant cutting-grown starts in full sun with sharp drainage and give them room to spread. Bred for durability, this selection keeps blooming through summer heat that halts older varieties. Bright magenta flower heads carpet the plant from spring through frost with little care.

planting.note: A clean white form of the heat- and cold-tough EnduraScape series.

directions: Set rooted starts level in full sun with well-drained soil and avoid overwatering. This durable series flowers reliably through midsummer heat and returns in milder winters. Crisp white flower clusters bloom from spring to frost and blend easily with brighter companions.

planting.note: A hardy red-flowered trailing selection grown from cuttings.

directions: Plant rooted starts at soil level in full sun with excellent drainage and let stems trail and root. This mildew-resistant selection spreads into a low mat and takes heat and humidity in stride. Rich red flower clusters cover the plant from late spring to frost; shear lightly to keep it blooming.

planting.note: A soft-lavender trailing hybrid propagated from cuttings.

directions: Set rooted plants in full sun with fast drainage and space them to knit together into a mat. This vigorous hybrid resists mildew and keeps flowering through heat when given good air movement. Pale lavender flower heads bloom heavily from spring to frost with occasional shearing.

planting.note: A Western native best fall-sown so seed chills over winter.

directions: Sow onto the surface of loosened ground in fall and let winter moisture and cold ready the seed. This drought-adapted native takes full sun and dry summers once its roots reach down. Slender spikes of small blue-purple flowers open through spring and early summer and support native bees.

planting.note: A small dry-ground native; fall-sow for natural stratification.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface of lean soil in fall and let cold, moist winter conditions break dormancy. This compact native thrives on dry, rocky ground in full sun and needs little attention. Thin spikes of lavender flowers bloom from the base upward through early and midsummer.

planting.note: A woodland-edge native; fall sowing supplies the needed cold period.

directions: Sow seed on moist ground in fall and let winter cold and damp prepare it for spring sprouting. This tall native tolerates part shade and average to moist soil at woodland edges and clearings. Airy branched spikes carry tiny white flowers through summer that feed small pollinators.

planting.note: A robust strain of tall verbena sown early indoors.

directions: Surface-sow onto warm moist mix, leave uncovered, and wait through the uneven germination. Plant into lean, well-drained soil in full sun where the tall wiry stems weave through neighbors. Flat clusters of purple flowers hover on branching stems from midsummer to frost and reseed freely.

planting.note: A mildew-resistant trailing hybrid grown as a tender perennial from cuttings.

directions: Plant rooted starts level in full sun with good drainage, spacing them to fill baskets or beds. This vigorous series resists powdery mildew and keeps a tidy trailing habit all season. Deep red flower clusters bloom nonstop from spring to frost and rarely need deadheading.

planting.note: A pale-violet trailing hybrid set out after frost from cuttings.

directions: Set cutting-grown starts at soil level in full sun with free-draining soil and space to trail. The large-flowered series holds up to heat and resists mildew when air moves freely around it. Soft violet flower heads appear from spring through frost with almost no maintenance.

planting.note: A seed-grown lacy-leaved trailer treated as a tender perennial.

directions: Sow indoors onto warm moist mix, cover very lightly, and keep even until sprouts show. Move to full sun with sharp drainage where the finely divided foliage spreads into a trailing mat. Deep violet-purple flower clusters bloom from early summer to frost and it tolerates heat and drought well.

planting.note: A compact seed series of garden verbena grown as a tender perennial.

directions: Start seed indoors in warm mix, cover lightly, and keep the surface from drying while it sprouts. Set out in full sun with good drainage where the mounded plants stay neat and early to flower. Rounded clusters in a range of colors bloom from early summer through fall with light deadheading.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed for one to two months, then sow into moist prairie or rain-garden soil.

directions: Press seed onto damp soil in a sunny, seasonally wet spot and do not cover it deeply. This tidy prairie native tolerates clay and periodic flooding once established. Tight clusters of bright purple flowers open in mid to late summer atop sturdy, self-supporting stems.

planting.note: Cold-stratify then sow in spring, or direct-sow in fall for natural chilling.

directions: Surface-sow onto moist, fertile soil and keep it evenly damp during germination. The willowy, narrow-leaved stems carry unusually large, spidery purple flowerheads. Blooms appear in late summer and are a magnet for swallowtails and monarchs.

planting.note: Sow chilled seed in spring into well-drained, sunny ground.

directions: Press seed lightly into the soil surface in full sun, as this prairie species handles dry, rocky sites well. Fuzzy stems bear rounded clusters of purple flowers. Bloom peaks in mid to late summer, with curled bracts giving the seedheads a distinctive look.

planting.note: Direct-sow in fall or cold-stratify and sow in spring into moist soil.

directions: Sow onto the surface of damp, heavy soil where moisture lingers, such as a low meadow or pond edge. The woolly-stemmed plants tolerate clay and seasonal wet feet. Broad clusters of red-purple flowers open in late summer and hum with pollinators.

planting.note: Sow stratified seed in spring into sandy, well-drained soil.

directions: Surface-sow into lean, sandy ground, as this southeastern native prefers dry, sunny sites. The very narrow leaves give the plant an airy texture. Loose sprays of purple flowers appear from midsummer into fall.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed and sow in spring in part to full sun.

directions: Sow shallowly in humus-rich woodland-edge soil that stays lightly moist. This Appalachian species accepts more shade than most ironweeds. Flat clusters of purple flowers open in mid to late summer above broad, tapering leaves.

planting.note: Sow stratified seed in spring in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Press seed onto sandy or rocky soil in full sun, mimicking its native piedmont barrens. A basal rosette of leaves sends up nearly leafless flowering stalks, giving the plant its stemless look. Bright purple flowers appear from early to midsummer.

planting.note: Direct-sow in fall or cold-stratify and sow in spring in moist soil.

directions: Surface-sow into fertile, evenly moist soil in full sun. The soft, drooping foliage distinguishes this southeastern species. Airy purple flower clusters open in late summer and attract a wide range of butterflies.

planting.note: Set out container plants or divisions in spring once the soil warms.

directions: Plant at the same depth as the nursery pot in well-drained soil and water until established, after which it tolerates drought. The threadlike foliage forms a fine-textured, mounding cloud that never needs staking. A dense haze of violet-purple flowers covers the plant in late summer and early fall.

planting.note: Plant container-grown stock or divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Set plants at their original depth in well-drained soil and keep evenly moist the first season. This hybrid pairs fine, narrow foliage with a strong upright habit and good drought tolerance once established. Rich purple flowers open in late summer over a long bloom period, feeding many late-season pollinators.

planting.note: Plant divisions or potted stock in spring in moist, fertile soil.

directions: Give this large selection room and plant it at nursery depth in rich, evenly moist soil. The tall, willowy stems carry big, showy spidery flowerheads and stand well without support in a sheltered spot. Deep violet blooms open in late summer and make a bold statement at the back of a border.

planting.note: Set out container plants or divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Plant at the same level as the pot in fertile, well-drained soil and water through the first summer. The finely cut, ferny foliage gives a soft look and turns attractive in autumn. Purple flowers open in late summer and are followed by ornamental seedheads that carry interest into fall.

planting.note: Raise seedlings indoors and transplant to the border after frost danger passes.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of a moist tray and keep it lit and evenly damp until sprouting. Give this taller species room and support in windy sites. Tall tapering spikes of blue-violet bloom over a long midsummer stretch and draw many pollinators.

planting.note: Set out the low rosettes in spring in cool, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Sow shallowly and keep cool while germinating, then grow on the glossy evergreen rosettes. Plant where soil stays lightly moist and shade is offered in hot regions. Airy stems of pale sky-blue flowers rise in late spring above the mat of foliage.

planting.note: Plant into gritty, well-drained ground in spring for a spreading mat.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and keep it evenly moist until it sprouts. Grow in lean, sharply drained soil where it will knit into a low carpet ideal for rock gardens and edging. Short dense spikes of deep blue smother the foliage in late spring.

planting.note: Set plants out in spring in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Surface-sow and provide light and steady moisture until germination. This tough species thrives in dry, gritty soils once rooted and dislikes soggy winters. Vivid true-blue flowers cover the mounded plants in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Set divisions at the depth they grew before and water in until established. The trailing stems root as they spread and form a durable low mat with bronze-tinted foliage. Small sapphire-blue flowers with white eyes appear in spring, sometimes rebloooming lightly.

planting.note: Start indoors and move out after frost into full sun.

directions: Press the fine seed onto moist mix and keep it lit until it sprouts. Prized for its felted silver-gray leaves, it wants sharp drainage and full sun to keep its color. Blue-violet spikes rise above the silvery mound in early summer.

planting.note: Plant plugs or divisions in spring into lean, fast-draining soil.

directions: Space small plants closely and firm them into gritty ground for a flat, walkable carpet. This tough evergreen groundcover tolerates foot traffic and drought once settled. It disappears under a sheet of cobalt-blue flowers in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in dry, sunny, well-drained sites.

directions: Set divisions into poor, gritty soil where they will spread into a woolly gray mat. Extremely drought-tolerant, it suits banks, wall tops, and gaps between paving. Deep blue flowers with white centers blanket the mat in spring.

planting.note: Sow or plant in spring in a lightly shaded, informal setting.

directions: Scatter seed on firmed soil and press it in lightly, keeping the bed moist. This creeping woodland-edge species roots along the ground and naturalizes in grassy spots. Slim spikes of pale lilac-blue flowers appear from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Sow in spring in a wild or meadow-style planting.

directions: Press seed lightly into moist soil and keep evenly damp until it germinates. A spreading meadow plant, it self-sows and can wander, so give it a relaxed setting. Bright blue flowers with a white eye open in loose sprays in late spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions or plugs in spring in moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Press small divisions into evenly moist soil where the trailing stems can root and knit. It makes a fine flat lawn substitute or filler between stepping stones with light traffic. Tiny pale blue flowers dot the mossy green mat in spring.

planting.note: Plant into gritty, sharply drained soil in spring.

directions: Surface-sow and keep just moist until sprouts appear, then grow the ferny mats on the dry side. This alpine species wants full sun and excellent drainage to look its best. Intense deep-blue flowers cover the finely cut foliage in late spring.

planting.note: Stratify seed and plant out in spring in rich, moist ground.

directions: Chill the seed before surface-sowing and keep the tray lit until germination. This robust relative of Culver's root forms strong upright clumps in fertile, retentive soil. Broad spikes of lilac-blue to pale purple flowers rise in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant nursery divisions in spring in full sun and good drainage.

directions: Set plants at their original depth and water in well to establish the clump. Deadhead faded spikes to prompt fresh bloom and keep the compact habit tidy. Bright rosy-red flower spikes cover this dwarf selection from early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into sunny, well-drained beds.

directions: Firm plants into place and keep them watered through their first season. Removing spent spikes extends the display and neatens the plant. Long tapering spikes of pure white flowers appear from early through midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for a compact, tidy clump.

directions: Set the plant level with the soil and water it in to settle the roots. This selection stays dense and rarely needs staking, though shearing spent spikes prolongs color. Densely packed spires of deep violet-blue flowers hold for weeks in early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Plant into gritty soil and water gently until roots take hold. Its silvery foliage and low habit suit the front of a border or a rock garden. Short spikes of bright rose-pink flowers rise above the gray leaves in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with room for tall stems.

directions: Set divisions in fertile soil and stake in exposed sites to keep the tall spikes upright. Cut back after the first flush to encourage a lighter second showing. Long, elegant spires of clear lavender-blue flowers open in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun to light shade where the foliage shows.

directions: Plant divisions at soil level and keep them watered while establishing. The cream-and-green variegated leaves earn their keep even between bloom flushes, and a little afternoon shade prevents scorch. White flower spikes rise above the patterned foliage in summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun and average soil.

directions: Set plants level with the ground and water them in to settle. This compact selection blooms freely and reblooms well if sheared after the first flush. Soft blue flower spikes appear over a long stretch from early to late summer.

scientific_name: Veronica austriaca subsp. teucrium 'Crater Lake Blue'

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: Plant divisions into gritty, well-drained soil and avoid winter wet. Shear the mounded plants after flowering to keep them tidy and compact. Sheets of brilliant gentian-blue flowers cover the foliage in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil as a spreading mat.

directions: Set plants where the trailing stems can spread and root into a dense low carpet. The foliage flushes bronze-purple in cool weather, adding interest outside bloom time. Masses of deep sky-blue flowers with white eyes open in early spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in cool, evenly moist soil.

directions: Plant the low evergreen rosettes at soil level and keep them lightly moist. A touch of afternoon shade suits it best in warm regions. Slender stems of near-white flowers finely veined in pale blue rise in late spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun for bright foliage color.

directions: Plant into gritty, well-drained soil and give it full sun to hold the golden leaf color. It forms a low chartreuse mat that brightens rock gardens and edges. Short spikes of deep blue flowers contrast with the yellow foliage in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and average, well-drained soil.

directions: Set plants at their original depth and water in to establish. Deadhead spent spikes regularly and this award-winning hybrid will rebloom for months. Dense spikes of deep violet-blue flowers appear from early summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for a long-blooming, low mound.

directions: Plant into well-drained soil and water gently while it settles in. This compact hybrid blooms exceptionally long, especially with regular deadheading. Spikes of rich blue flowers cover the low mound from early summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring at the front of a sunny border.

directions: Firm the small plants into well-drained soil and water in. This very dwarf selection is ideal for edging, troughs, and rock gardens. Short chunky spikes of bright rosy-pink flowers appear over a long summer season.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and moderately fertile soil.

directions: Set plants level with the soil and keep them watered while rooting. Cutting back spent flower spikes encourages a strong repeat display. Large, densely packed spikes of reddish-purple flowers appear through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for months of bloom.

directions: Plant into well-drained soil and water in to establish the clump. Shear spent spikes to keep this long bloomer producing fresh flowers. Full spikes of vivid purple flowers cover the plant from early through late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and average soil.

directions: Set the plant at soil level and water in until established. Regular deadheading keeps this compact hybrid blooming for a long stretch. Spikes of bright rosy-pink flowers appear from early summer into early fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, well-drained spot as a low mat.

directions: Plant into gritty soil where the trailing stems can spread and root. The foliage takes on bronze-purple tints in cool weather for extra interest. Soft blue flowers open over the low mat in spring, often with scattered repeat bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with room for tall stems.

directions: Set divisions in fertile soil and support them in windy locations. Cut back after the first flush to promote a second round of spikes. Long spires of soft lavender-blue flowers open in midsummer and draw butterflies.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant the evergreen rosettes at soil level and keep them evenly moist. This vigorous selection forms sturdier clumps than the species. Airy stems of pale sky-blue flowers rise above the foliage in late spring.
